# The Benefits of Real-Time Continuous Analysis

Aug 8, 2022 | [Blog](https://www.hidenanalytical.com/category/blog/)

Real-time continuous analysis is utilised in laboratories all over the world to improve safety, efficiency, and testing capabilities of chemical compounds. For example, quadrupole mass spectrometry is capable of resolving gas mixtures comprising numerous constituents in real time.

Using real-time continuous analytics to identify and analyse chemical compounds will provide numerous advantages to a business. No longer will you be waiting hours, or even days, for data to be processed and readily available. With real-time analysis, you can analyse data almost instantly, without even having to request it.

**Benefits of Real-Time Continuous Analysis**

Apart from real-time results, additional benefits include:

![](https://www.hidenanalytical.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/ScreenHunter-73.png)

**Hiden Analytical**

Hiden Analytical manufactures quadrupole mass spectrometers for a wide range of applications. These applications include gas and vapour analysis, residual gas analysis, thermal and surface analysis and more. The software we supply with our mass spectrometers allow for real-time data analysis, immediate exporting of data and a range of additional features.

One of the [software packages we provide](https://www.hidenanalytical.com/products/software/qga-2/) is the QGA 2, which has been designed specifically for quantitative gas and vapour analysis. This application-specific software can monitor up to 32 species via real-time continuous analysis.

The benefits of the QGA 2 software include:

If you would like more information about our QGA 2 software, or to request a quote, please get in touch with us via the contact form.

--- SOURCE 2: A systematic review of real-time data monitoring and its potential ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039

SUMMARY:
The review also showed that applying real-time monitoring and dynamic environmental assessment in the food sector is a novel concept that could be used to improve the data collection process. Dynamic Life Cycle Assessment (DLCA) is an approach that offers several advantages over traditional static LCAs. By considering temporal variations, incorporating real-time data, and integrating adaptive management practices, dynamic LCA enhances the accuracy, relevance, and applicability of environmental assessments (Levasseur et al., 2010a, Levasseur et al., 2010b). Collecting dynamic data requires access to real-time or near real-time information on various parameters, which can be challenging due to limitations in data availability and accessibility, posing challenges to the accurate and timely assessment of environmental impacts (Beloin-Saint-Pierre et al., 2020b).

FULL CONTENT:

A systematic review of real-time data monitoring and its potential application to support dynamic life cycle inventories
========================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Tamíris Pacheco da Costa a b, Daniele Mesquita Bordalo da Costa c, Fionnuala Murphy a b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eiar.2024.107416](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eiar.2024.107416 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0195925524000039&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •IoT enables automatic data collection for improved [life cycle inventories](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/life-cycle-inventory "Learn more about life cycle inventories from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •The integration of IoT and LCA can quantify environmental impacts in novel ways. 
*   •It enables predictive actions and can be extended to challenging sectors. 
*   •IoT technologies have exponential growth potential for LCA studies. 
*   •IoT and LCA integration can lead to better decision-making in the food supply chain. 

Abstract
--------

Dynamic monitoring is crucial for improving the performance of industrial systems. It allows real-time data collection, contributing to more proactive planning and early detection of issues before they cause unplanned downtime and revenue loss. [Monitoring systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monitoring-system "Learn more about Monitoring systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), particularly sensor and Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, are growing exponentially and can become an enormous source of information. This information could also support dynamic [life cycle assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/life-cycle-assessment "Learn more about life cycle assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LCA) to quantify potential environmental impacts in a novel way. However, although real-time monitoring provides new opportunities in different domains, it also presents some environmental challenges that must be addressed, such as substantial electricity consumption and the use of [batteries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This study reviews the use of dynamic [monitoring techniques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monitoring-technique "Learn more about monitoring techniques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in LCA studies and how they can be applied to monitor real-time environmental aspects in different domains. A systematic literature review was conducted, encompassing academic documents published within peer-reviewed scientific journals. A total of 34 articles focused on how real-time data is being addressed in LCA studies were selected. The review showed that combining LCA and real-time sensors could result in an improved decision-making tool based on a more holistic view of all the factors relevant to the products or processes. However, the trade-offs of using real-time monitoring technologies to support the development of [life cycle inventories](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/life-cycle-inventory "Learn more about life cycle inventories from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") need to be widely assessed to be used as a basis for the implementation in different sectors. The review also showed that applying real-time monitoring and dynamic [environmental assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-assessment "Learn more about environmental assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the food sector is a novel concept that could be used to improve the data collection process. Two articles identified in the review showed the use of real-time data in the [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sectors, being used as a tool to monitor the quality and safety of the products. This could be especially relevant to improving and optimising the traceability and safety of food supply chains while reducing environmental impacts.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925523003797)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S019592552300375X)

Keywords
--------

Dynamic inventory

Life cycle assessment

Data-driven LCA

Real-time monitoring

Sensors

Traceability

1. Introduction
---------------

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) has become a pivotal instrument for appraising environmental performance across diverse industries. Over the past few years, an increasing necessity has arisen for a more precise and prompt evaluation of environmental consequences across the entire life cycle of products ([Basic et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0015); [Hagen et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0120), [Hagen et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0125)). However, traditional LCAs have typically relied on static data inputs ([Bidstrup, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0050); [van der Harst and Potting, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0130); [Wang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0295)), which may not capture temporal variations and real-time changes in the system.

Traditional data collection approaches are considerably time and effort-consuming because of the structural complexity of processes in the industry, which can lead to human errors. In addition, the focus on the lifetime of a project may not align with the [full life cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/full-life-cycle "Learn more about full life cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") perspective of other methodologies ([Hoogmartens et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0135)). This complexity is one of the factors that makes LCA studies challenging to conduct within the business because it necessitates specialists capable of conducting the analysis, a collaborative attitude of management, and factory staff able to collect primary data efficiently ([Linhares and Pereira, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0170)). Moreover, most LCA studies depend on databases within software systems, like the [Ecoinvent database](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ecoinvent-database "Learn more about Ecoinvent database from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Wernet et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0310)), sourced from averaged information across diverse sectors or gathered manually from literature. However, these databases fail to be satisfactory because they lack real-time, dynamic, and precise data ([Wernet et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0310)).

Dynamic Life Cycle Assessment (DLCA) is an approach that offers several advantages over traditional static LCAs. By considering temporal variations, incorporating real-time data, and integrating [adaptive management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/adaptive-management "Learn more about adaptive management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") practices, dynamic LCA enhances the accuracy, relevance, and applicability of [environmental assessments](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-assessment "Learn more about environmental assessments from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Levasseur et al., 2010a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0160), [Levasseur et al., 2010b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0165)). The term “dynamic LCA” can be interpreted in various ways, and each interpretation has distinct implications for the scope of the study. It is possible to find three main types of dynamism in DLCA studies, dynamic system boundary, dynamic inventory, and dynamic modelling/characterisation ([Sohn et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0235), [Sohn et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0240)).

The first dynamic LCA relates to considering temporal variations in system boundaries. Traditional static LCAs typically define a fixed system boundary for the assessment, assuming constant inputs and outputs. In contrast, dynamic LCA considers changes in system boundaries over time, capturing variations in processes, technologies, and materials used throughout the life cycle. Using a dynamic system boundary support captures inherent variations, periods of occurrence, or evolution within the temporal scope of models' components ([Sohn et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0235), [Sohn et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0240)). This temporal dimension allows for a more comprehensive assessment of environmental impacts, particularly in systems where inputs or outputs fluctuate significantly.

In a dynamic inventory, time-differentiated data is considered to describe the [elementary flows](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/elementary-flow "Learn more about elementary flows from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), reflecting changes in the environmental performance of a product or system. This approach enables the evaluation of time-sensitive factors such as energy consumption, emissions, and resource use, allowing for a more accurate representation of the environmental impacts over time ([Sohn et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0235), [Sohn et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0240)).

Dynamic modelling, where characterisation factors are adjusted to reflect a difference in temporal scale ([Sohn et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0235), [Sohn et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0240)). In traditional static LCA, characterisation factors are often based on average values that assume a fixed temporal scale. However, environmental impacts can vary significantly depending on the time frame in which they occur. Dynamic characterisation recognises this variability and adjusts the factors accordingly, considering the time-dependent nature of emissions and resource consumption. One example of dynamic characterisation is the consideration of the time-dependent impacts of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. In static LCA, GHG emissions are often characterised based on their [Global Warming Potential](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming-potential "Learn more about Global Warming Potential from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (GWP) over a fixed time horizon, typically 100 years. However, the climate impact of GHG emissions varies depending on the temporal scale [Su et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0260). Short-lived climate pollutants, such as methane, have a higher impact in the short term but decay more rapidly than long-lived pollutants like [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The dynamic characterisation can adjust the characterisation factors to reflect this difference, allowing a more accurate representation of the time-dependent climate impacts.

Collecting dynamic data requires access to real-time or near real-time information on various parameters, which can be challenging due to limitations in data availability and accessibility, posing challenges to the accurate and timely assessment of environmental impacts ([Beloin-Saint-Pierre et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0045)). Integrating real-time [data monitoring](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monitoring-data "Learn more about data monitoring from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with dynamic LCAs offers a promising approach to address these limitations and provide more comprehensive and accurate environmental assessments.

Real-time monitoring technologies can have great potential and credibility in handling the abovementioned problems, such as sensors, [laser scanners](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/laser-scanner "Learn more about laser scanners from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), barcode identification equipment, mobile, [global positioning systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/global-positioning-system "Learn more about global positioning systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and other information sensing equipment ([Ingrao et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0145); [Yang et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0320)). These technologies can be employed to collect real-time data at each stage of a product to manage resources efficiently and intelligently ([An et al., 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0005), [An et al., 2021b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0010)). The information can be collected, and a detailed database can be built quickly and dynamically to monitor and continuously improve the entire life cycle of the product ([Ray, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0220)). Combining these technologies with life cycle assessment may allow novel ways to identify and quantify environmental impacts. This information is particularly relevant for industries, including importers, exporters, container manufacturers, and retailers, since these stakeholders frequently have different interests ([Wu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0315)). While DLCA introduces novel dynamism in system boundaries, inventory, and modelling/characterisation aspects, applying real-time monitoring technologies such as the [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) in LCA studies is novel.

Hence, there is a need to systematically and comprehensively explore the possible uses of real-time monitoring and its potential role in enhancing LCA by advancing data collection methods. To achieve this goal, this study reviews how real-time data collection has been addressed in LCA studies, including the impact of IoT technology. The review aims to present how the scientific literature has addressed the acquisition of dynamic [life cycle inventories](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/life-cycle-inventory "Learn more about life cycle inventories from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and how the knowledge obtained so far can be expanded to other relevant sectors, such as the food supply chains, focusing on how dynamic inventories have been included in LCA studies. This study's applications extend to a diverse audience, including industry professionals, technology innovators, policymakers, and academic researchers. It offers insights for improving [environmental impact assessments](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about environmental impact assessments from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [life cycle analyses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/life-cycle-analysis "Learn more about life cycle analyses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across various sectors.

2. Literature search
--------------------

The process of identifying studies within scientific journals was conducted using a six-step approach, adhering to the guidelines set by the Preferred Reporting Items for [Systematic reviews](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about Systematic reviews from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines ([Page et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0205)), as presented in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#f0005). The model demands a clear identification, comprehensive screening, defined eligibility, detailed inclusion, thorough synthesis, and transparent reporting for systematic literature reviews. First, the review was conducted by searching for studies published in peer-reviewed indexed journals in an electronic database in the last 20 years. The articles were first systematically screened via the Web of Science search engine. Only literature reported in English was included in the review scope.

![Image 8: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (300KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (300KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Flow diagram of the literature review.

The screening of scientific articles combined the search terms (“life cycle assessment” or “LCA”) and (“dynamic” or “real-time” or “DLCA” and “sensor”) on titles, abstracts, and keywords, resulting in a total of 426 potentially relevant articles. After excluding review articles, book chapters, and grey literature, 345 articles were identified. The additional screening to evaluate the relevance of the identified articles resulted in the permanence of 25 articles. The relevance of each article was assessed by screening the abstracts to identify the dynamism of the study. DLCA can be found in studies where relevant temporal and/or spatial differentiation of flows are explicitly defined to quantify environmental impacts. It is possible to find three main types of dynamism in DLCA studies: dynamic system boundary, dynamic inventory and dynamic modelling/characterisation.

Therefore, based on the objective of this paper, only studies using a dynamic inventory were included, where time-differentiated data is considered to describe [elementary flows](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/elementary-flow "Learn more about elementary flows from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), e.g. field monitoring, simulation, and prediction based on [historical data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/historical-data "Learn more about historical data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). If there were uncertainties regarding articles based solely on the abstracts, a more extensive examination of the complete paper was undertaken. This rigorous evaluation ensured that all relevant articles meeting the study's requirements were retained. DLCA studies investigating spatial differentiation fall outside the scope of this study, as the primary focus is to examine the impact and effectiveness of real-time technologies in the context of LCA. As such, the research specifically concentrated on the dynamic aspects of LCA, particularly related to real-time data collection, analysis, and decision-making. Articles addressing frameworks focused on the dynamic inventory stage were also integrated into the review scope and studies based on simulation or predicted data inventories.

Nine additional articles were identified from citations within the previously identified articles, resulting in 34 for the quantitative analysis. It is important to note that the specific search terms were not necessarily included in their titles, abstracts, or keywords. However, the potential relevance of these articles was recognised based on their citation context within the previously identified literature.

3. Results
----------

### 3.1. Dynamism in life cycle inventories

The life cycle inventory (LCI) plays a central role in LCA studies because its fundamental purpose is quantifying material and energy flows across the system evaluated ([Ekvall et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0100)). The delicate nature of LCI originates from the essential need to develop datasets that are as realistic of real-world systems as possible. For each operation unit, site-specific data should be collected and validated in terms of quality and reliability. However, a substantial volume of data is typically necessary to establish a reliable model of a system ([Su et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0255)). In this regard, [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#f0010) presents the efforts from the literature to overcome the challenges regarding the development of reliable and real-time LCI over the years, according to the approach taken in each of them.

![Image 9: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (161KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (161KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0195925524000039-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Time series and types of reviewed publications.

The oldest publication selected in the review is from 2011 and the most recent is from 2021. This could be due to increased data availability, technological advances and commercial databases to conduct LCA studies, such as [Ecoinvent](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ecoinvent "Learn more about Ecoinvent from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The paper “Considering time in LCA” by [Levasseur et al., 2010a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0160), [Levasseur et al., 2010b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#bb0165) may have inspired the growing interest in temporal aspects, cited several times by the selected papers since 2010. However, the article itself was excluded from this analysis, as the research focus centered on including empirical studies that directly investigated the specific aspects of dynamic LCAs. As such, review articles, which primarily synthesise and analyse existing literature, were not within the scope of this analysis.

The selected articles can be categorised into three groups: [case study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), methodological framework, or framework applied to a case study. Framework development applied to a case study is the most common approach (_n_=20). These studies focus on quantitative assessment, frequently introducing a framework initially and subsequently illustrating its practical use through a case study. The second largest group summarises publications with practical examples (_n_=8), while purely methodological frameworks, where the research proposes ideas at a conceptual level without extensive practical case implementation, continue to have limited impact over time (_n_=6). All reviewed articles and the product application are listed in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0195925524000039#t0005).

# Unlocking Material Properties: Advanced Analytics in Materials Informatics

Materials informatics, a field that merges materials science with data analytics, stands at the forefront of modern scientific endeavors. As we venture into an era where data-driven decisions are pivotal, analytics in the realm of materials informatics gains unprecedented importance. The analytical tools and data models prevalent in this domain facilitate a nuanced understanding of material properties. This article aims to dissect the types of analytics and data models commonly employed in materials informatics, serving as a comprehensive guide for scientists and researchers interested in deepening their understanding of material properties through data-centric approaches.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66aa1ca0bb25c74dde4d48f5/66caddc50b23652cea5bf59e_65f85556c45db2535f1e3b4b_finger-pressing-digital-graphic.jpeg)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/66a76467298c454de539f7aa/66aa94e6ccea27a45533a11d_Texture.avif)

## Understanding Material Properties

Material properties are the inherent characteristicsâmechanical, thermal, electrical, and moreâthat dictate how a material will behave under various conditions. These attributes can be complex and multi-dimensional, requiring robust scientific methods for comprehension and manipulation. This is where materials informatics shines; the field leverages computational methods and analytics to decode the labyrinth of data, leading to more accurate predictions and better material design.

## Types of Analytics in Materials Informatics

### Descriptive Analytics

Descriptive analytics in materials informatics serve as a foundational layer, helping scientists understand historical material behavior by scrutinizing past data. The aim is to extract meaningful insights that help in interpreting trends, patterns, or anomalies in material properties. For example, descriptive analytics can illuminate the correlation between temperature resistance and the crystalline structure in certain alloys.

### Predictive Analytics

Predictive analytics takes materials informatics a notch higher by not just describing what has happened but predicting what could happen in the future. By employing machine learning algorithms and statistical models, predictive analytics can forecast material behaviors under varying conditions. Researchers often use this form of analytics to predict material fatigue, corrosion rates, and other critical attributes, thereby aiding in the development of more durable and efficient materials.

### Prescriptive Analytics

The most advanced form of analytics, prescriptive analytics, offers actionable insights and recommended courses of action based on the data. Scientists can use prescriptive models to determine the optimal pathways for material synthesis or modification. For instance, prescriptive analytics can recommend the best method to alloy two metals to achieve desired tensile strength while minimizing cost.

## Commonly Used Data Models in Materials Informatics

### Regression Models

Regression models are commonly used for quantifying relationships between variables, making them invaluable for understanding material properties like tensile strength or thermal conductivity. These models offer a mathematical equation that represents the correlation between different material attributes, which can be useful in both R&D and industrial applications.

### Classification Models

Classification models are tailored to categorize materials into different classes based on their properties. For instance, a classification model could distinguish between conductive and insulating materials based on a range of attributes such as resistivity, molecular structure, and more. These models play a crucial role in the initial stages of material selection for various applications.

### Clustering Models

Clustering models help in the grouping of materials based on similarities in their properties. Researchers often employ these models to discover new classes of materials or to identify outliers that may exhibit exceptional or unique characteristics. The primary value of clustering lies in its ability to unearth hidden patterns or characteristics that may not be apparent through traditional methods.

## The Interplay of Analytics and Data Models in Materials Informatics

Analytics and data models are interwoven intricately in the fabric of materials informatics. Analytics provide the methodology to interpret and use data effectively, while data models offer the underlying structure that enables such analytics. The synergistic relationship between these elements amplifies our ability to understand complex material properties. For example, prescriptive analytics could utilize regression models to recommend changes in the chemical composition of a material to achieve specific mechanical properties.

## The Future of Analytics in Materials Informatics

As the field of materials informatics evolves, emerging technologies like quantum computing and neural networks promise to further refine our analytical capabilities. Enhanced computational power could facilitate more accurate simulations and predictions, thereby significantly advancing our understanding of material properties. The incorporation of real-time data analytics could also pave the way for adaptive materials that can modify their properties in response to environmental factors.

## Conclusion

In summary, analytics and data models form the cornerstone of materials informatics, enabling a sophisticated understanding of material properties. The types of analyticsâdescriptive, predictive, and prescriptiveâalong with a variety of data models like regression, classification, and clustering models, contribute to this nuanced comprehension. As we stand on the brink of technological innovations that promise to further evolve the field, continuous exploration and research are essential. The potent combination of analytics and data models in materials informatics is not merely a technological advancement but a paradigm shift in how we approach materials science.

## Breadcrumb

# Real-Time Machine Learning in Experimental Materials Science

Faculty Mentor/s:  Joshua Agar, Assistant Professor, Materials Science & Engineering

Students on the team this summer: 

Oluwafolajinmi Olugbodi – 2023 – UG - Computer Engineering

Tri Nguyen – 2021 – UG - Materials Science & Engineering

William Reichard-Flynn – 2021 – UG - Earth and Environmental Science Masters

Andrew Zheng – 2024 – UG - Mechanical Engineering

Project Description: In materials science and physics more broadly there is a growing trend to conduct multimodal experiments (experiments that collect data from a variety of sources). The boon in data collection has left a majority of the data collected under-analyzed leaving important physics left undiscovered. This project will develop machine and deep learning methods to discover actionable information from such data. This project will also consider how such models can be implemented on specialty AI hardware for real-time analysis. The work will focus on materials problems as they provide unique ways to stress-test practical theories of machine and deep learning. Outcomes of this work have direct impacts on creating interpretable AI, controlling fairness and bias, and creating autonomous control systems. The impacts of these theories can be adapted to solve problems in medicine and healthcare, resource management and logistics, and manufacturing and processing.

Month/Year Project Began:JUNE 2020

enriched_points.expanded_content: Real-time analysis enables scientists to observe and characterize dynamic processes, allowing immediate adjustments and informed decisions based on the latest data, enhancing experimental accuracy.

Real-time monitoring reveals how environmental factors influence material properties, aiding in the development of stronger, more durable materials through data-driven insights and predictive modeling.
By integrating real-time techniques, research accelerates, fostering innovative discoveries and efficient methodologies that push scientific boundaries and improve overall experimental outcomes.
